files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.AddDeviceBenchmarkEvent
files.Summary: Adds a new device to the engine at the scheduled tick, simulating a node joining the network mid-simulation.

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.BenchmarkConfig
files.Summary:
Fully serialisable description of a benchmark scenario.

A defines everything needed to reproduce a headless simulation run deterministically:

  • Simulation engine settings (visibility, TTL, packet travel time).
  • The initial network layout as a list of .
  • A timeline of items that modify the network at specific ticks (toggle, add, remove device).
  • The total number of ticks to simulate.
  • The list of router names to test; will run one full simulation per router and collect results for comparison.

Instances are stored as JSON together with their results so a test can be replayed or shared.

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.BenchmarkDeviceKind
files.Summary: Discriminator for the device kind stored in a benchmark configuration.

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.BenchmarkEvent
files.Summary: Base type for all events that can be scheduled in a .

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.BenchmarkEventEntry
files.Summary: Associates a with the engine tick at which it should be fired during a benchmark run.

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.BenchmarkResult
files.Summary: The collected statistics from one headless simulation run for a single router protocol.

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.BenchmarkRunProgress
files.Summary: Progress snapshot emitted while a benchmark is running.

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.BenchmarkRunner
files.Summary:
Executes a headlessly - without any UI - and produces a containing one per configured router.

Threading: supports both synchronous and asynchronous execution. The async entry point periodically yields to keep UI render loops responsive on single-threaded environments such as Blazor WebAssembly. Each router run uses an isolated engine/statistics context so independent runs can execute in parallel when requested.

Progress reporting: the optional onProgress callback in receives detailed snapshots while each router is running, including per-router tick progress and overall progress.

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.BenchmarkSession
files.Summary: The complete, serialisable record of one benchmark execution. Stored as JSON so users can save, share, and reload results.

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.DeviceBenchmarkDto
files.Summary: Serialisable snapshot of a single device's configuration used inside . Converted to a live by when it builds the engine state. Declared as a record so Blazor event handlers can use with expressions to create modified copies without mutating shared state.

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.RemoveDeviceBenchmarkEvent
files.Summary: Removes the named device from the engine at the scheduled tick, simulating a node failure or graceful leave.

files.Title: Engine.Benchmark.ToggleBenchmarkEvent
files.Summary: Hub sends a toggle (on/off) to the named emitter device at the scheduled tick.

files.Title: Engine.Core.DeviceRegisteredEventArgs
files.Summary: Event args for .

files.Title: Engine.Core.DeviceRemovedEventArgs
files.Summary: Event args for .

files.Title: Engine.Core.PacketDeliveredEventArgs
files.Summary: Event args for (reached its destination).

files.Title: Engine.Core.PacketExpiredEventArgs
files.Summary: Event args for (TTL reached zero).

files.Title: Engine.Core.PacketLimitExceededException
files.Summary:
Thrown by when the number of in-flight packets in the priority queue reaches or exceeds .

This exception signals that the simulation has entered an unstable state (packet storm / routing loop) where more packets are being generated than the network can drain. The host should catch this exception, stop the tick loop, and surface the error to the user.

files.Title: Engine.Core.PacketRegisteredEventArgs
files.Summary: Event args for .

files.Title: Engine.Core.SimulationEngine
files.Summary:
The central simulation engine type that drives the entire simulation. It maintains the device registry, owns the tick-based time model, and manages the priority queue of in-flight packets.

Every call to advances the simulation by one step: the tick counter is incremented, wall-clock delta time is measured, all subscribers to are notified, and any packets whose has been reached are dequeued and dispatched to their device.

Routing strategy and network-formation strategy are fully replaceable at runtime via and . The read-only topology view is exposed through .

files.Title: Engine.Core.TickedEventArgs
files.Summary: Event args for .

files.Title: Engine.Devices.Device
files.Summary:
Abstract base class for all devices in the simulation network. A device has a unique identity and a position in 2D space. When it receives a packet it either forwards it (if it is not the intended recipient) or accepts and processes it.

Forwarding is delegated to so that the active is always consulted. Devices never reference a concrete router directly.

files.Title: Engine.Devices.EmitterDevice
files.Summary:
A device that receives command packets from the hub and applies a boolean state to itself (e.g. turning an actuator on or off).

When is greater than zero the hub automatically sends a to this device every ticks, toggling its state from on to off and back. Set to 0 to disable automatic control signals.

Flooding note: with a unicast is re-broadcast at every intermediate hop, creating an exponential number of clones (approximately neighbours^TTL in the worst case). The TTL is therefore capped at rather than the default packet TTL. When a unicast router is active this has no extra cost because the packet follows a single path.

files.Title: Engine.Devices.GeneratorDevice
files.Summary:
A device that periodically generates sensor-like data packets and routes them toward the hub.

Routing is delegated to so the active strategy is always used.

files.Title: Engine.Devices.HubDevice
files.Summary:
Represents the central hub (gateway) device of the simulation network. The hub is the designated destination for packets generated by instances and the root of the network topology. It is registered as the singleton hub inside when added to the simulation.

The hub also emits protocol control traffic:

  • BEACON messages to seed DOWN-tree parent selection.
  • DECAY messages to prevent unbounded charge growth.

files.Title: Engine.Devices.NeighborState
files.Summary: Tracks protocol neighbor metadata used by charge-based routing and parent selection.

files.Title: Engine.Packets.BeaconPacket
files.Summary: Broadcast gateway beacon used to seed and maintain DOWN-tree convergence.

files.Title: Engine.Packets.BeaconPayload
files.Summary: Payload for BEACON messages.

files.Title: Engine.Packets.ConfirmationPacket
files.Summary: A special packet that is automatically generated and sent back to the original sender when a packet with set to true is successfully delivered. The payload contains the bytes of the confirmed packet, allowing the sender to correlate the acknowledgement with the original transmission.

files.Title: Engine.Packets.ControlPacket
files.Summary:
A command packet sent from the to a specific to set its on/off state.

The hub generates instances automatically at the interval configured on each via . When an receives this packet it toggles its to .

files.Title: Engine.Packets.DecayPacket
files.Summary: Broadcast packet carrying a monotonic decay epoch and damping factor.

files.Title: Engine.Packets.DecayPayload
files.Summary: Payload for DECAY messages.

files.Title: Engine.Packets.Packet
files.Summary:
Represents a unit of data travelling through the simulation network. A packet knows its originator (), its intended destination (), and the device it will be delivered to on the next simulation tick. Travel time is expressed in engine ticks via , and each hop decrements the counter - a packet that reaches zero TTL is silently dropped.

The same class also carries a swarm protocol envelope:

  • Routing metadata: version, previous-hop MAC, advertised charge and decay hint.
  • Secure header fields: direction, message type, origin/destination MAC, message id and sequence.
  • End-to-end tag bytes used by HMAC-SHA256 truncation.
These fields are optional for legacy routing and are populated by default for newly created packets.

files.Title: Engine.Packets.PacketAddress
files.Summary: Helpers for fixed-width 6-byte mesh addresses used by protocol headers.

files.Title: Engine.Packets.PacketData
files.Summary: Carries the application-level payload of a . The property is intentionally untyped so that any value (sensor readings, command flags, raw bytes, etc.) can be transported without the engine imposing a schema.

files.Title: Engine.Packets.PacketDirection
files.Summary: Direction of a protocol frame in the swarm mesh.

files.Title: Engine.Packets.SwarmMessageType
files.Summary: Message type registry for the swarm protocol envelope.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.FloodingPacketRouter
files.Summary:
Implements a flooding broadcast routing strategy.

When asked to route a packet the router queries the supplied for all devices visible to the sender and enqueues a cloned copy addressed to the nearest visible neighbors in a deterministic top-k window. Flooding uses visibility - not established connections - because it is inherently a broadcast mechanism that needs no prior topology knowledge.

This class is no longer a singleton. The active router instance is stored on and is injected into every routing call automatically by . To change the routing strategy at runtime replace with a different implementation.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.FullMeshNetworkBuilder
files.Summary:
A network-formation strategy that connects every pair of mutually visible devices, producing a full-visibility mesh.

This mirrors the original implicit behaviour of the engine where visibility and connectivity were equivalent: if two devices can see each other they are considered connected.

Note: as the number of devices grows, the number of edges grows as O(n?). For large networks consider a limited-degree or spanning-tree builder instead.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.IMutableNetworkTopology
files.Summary:
Extends with mutation methods so that an can update the connection graph without exposing mutation to routers or other consumers.

Only implementations and should hold a reference to . All other code (routers, statistics, UI) works through the read-only projection.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.INetworkBuilder
files.Summary:
Defines the contract for a network-formation (mesh-building) strategy.

A network builder is responsible for deciding which devices establish explicit connections with each other, forming the logical mesh topology. It operates on top of raw visibility data and produces an that the active then uses for forwarding decisions.

Examples of algorithms that implement this interface:

  • Full-mesh - every visible pair is connected (current implicit behaviour).
  • Limited-degree mesh - each device connects to at most K neighbours.
  • Minimum spanning tree - minimises total edge weight.
  • Cluster-head / LEACH-style hierarchy.

Like , the active builder is stored on and can be swapped at runtime from the UI to compare how different formation strategies affect routing statistics.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.INetworkTopology
files.Summary:
Represents the logical topology of the network at a given point in time.

The topology separates two concepts that are intentionally kept distinct:

  • Visibility Whether two devices are within radio range of each other. Used for broadcast/discovery and as a precondition for establishing a connection. Determined purely by Euclidean distance and .
  • Connection An explicit, mutually agreed logical link between two devices. A connection can only exist between mutually visible devices, but not every visible device is necessarily connected (subject to per-device connection limits, protocol decisions, etc.). Unicast routing and network-topology algorithms operate on connections, not on raw visibility.

Implementations are owned and updated by the active and are passed to on every routing call so that routers never need to touch directly.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.IPacketRouter
files.Summary:
Defines the contract for a packet routing strategy.

A router receives a packet from a forwarding device and is responsible for deciding which device(s) the packet should be sent to next. Implementations may use different strategies: flooding broadcast, shortest-path unicast, gradient routing, etc.

The active router is set on and is consulted by every device whenever it needs to forward a packet. Swapping the router at runtime (e.g. from UI) immediately changes routing behaviour for all subsequent packets, which enables side-by-side protocol comparison on the same network.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.KNearestNetworkBuilder
files.Summary: Builds a visibility-constrained k-nearest undirected mesh.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.MinimumSpanningTreeNetworkBuilder
files.Summary:
Builds a visibility-constrained minimum spanning forest.

For each connected visibility component this builder creates exactly n-1 links (tree), reducing redundant edges while preserving reachability inside the component.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.NetworkTopology
files.Summary:
Default in-memory implementation of .

Visibility is computed on-the-fly from device positions and ; it is never cached so it always reflects the current layout.

Connections are stored as an adjacency set (both directions) and are managed exclusively by the active .

files.Title: Engine.Routers.SmartFloodingPacketRouter
files.Summary:
An improved flooding router that reduces redundant packet copies by applying three optimisations over the naive :

  1. Bounded candidate set Routing decisions are made only on the nearest visible neighbors for deterministic fairness across protocols.
  2. Direct delivery If the intended destination () is directly visible from sender, the packet is sent only to that device - no unnecessary flood copies are made.
  3. No reverse path The packet is never forwarded back to (the device that just sent it here). This eliminates the most common source of duplicate copies in bidirectional flooding.
  4. No source echo The packet is never forwarded back to (the original source device), preventing useless round-trips.

Together these rules drastically shrink the clone fan-out compared to the naive flood, especially for unicast packets such as , while still guaranteeing delivery in any connected topology with sufficient TTL.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.SwarmProtocolPacketRouter
files.Summary:
Protocol-oriented router that combines charge-based UP forwarding and tree-first DOWN forwarding.

Behavior summary:

  • Visibility: route decisions are limited to nearest visible top-k neighbors.
  • UP bootstrap: until q_up signal appears, fan-out over the same top-k window as flooding.
  • UP converged: choose one best visible neighbor by observed q_up.
  • DOWN unicast: direct if destination is visible; otherwise forward to eligible children first.
  • DOWN broadcast: forward to eligible children; fallback to one best q_total neighbor.
  • All clones carry previous-hop MAC and charge metadata for neighbor learning.

files.Title: Engine.Routers.SwarmProtocolVector
files.Summary: Runtime-tunable parameter vector for the swarm protocol implementation. Values are aligned with the protocol tuning guide and Python batch model.

files.Title: Engine.Statistics.SimulationStatistics
files.Summary:
Statistics collector that listens to events and accumulates simulation metrics.

Extensibility: add a new to and subscribe to any event to populate it. The event is raised after every change so UI layers can re-render reactively without polling.

Every tick a is appended to (capped at entries) for time-series charts.

files.Title: Engine.Statistics.StatMetric
files.Summary: A single observable metric tracked by . UI layers bind to and listen to for re-renders.

files.Title: Engine.Statistics.TickSnapshot
files.Summary: An immutable snapshot of all plottable metric values captured at the end of a single engine tick. Stored in the history ring-buffer and consumed by the chart UI.
